question: Explain the primary purpose of the 'static' keyword in Java when applied to a variable. Describe the memory implications and a common use-case.
expectedPoints: A static variable belongs to the class itself, not to any specific instance (object).
There is only one copy of a static variable in memory, shared by all instances of the class.
A common use-case is for defining constants (e.g., static final) or maintaining a count of the number of instances created.

question: Compare and contrast a static method with an instance method. Detail the key restrictions placed on static methods and explain why these restrictions exist.
expectedPoints: A static method is called on the class (ClassName.method()), while an instance method is called on an object instance (object.method()).
Static methods cannot directly access instance variables or instance methods; they can only directly access other static members (variables/methods).
This restriction exists because static methods are not associated with an instance, so there is no 'this' reference for them to use to access instance-specific data.

question: A developer is designing a utility class for mathematical operations. Justify why this class should be designed with a private constructor and contain only static methods. Furthermore, explain what would happen if the class was declared as abstract instead and discuss the pitfalls of that approach.
expectedPoints: A utility class with only static methods is not meant to be instantiated. A private constructor prevents anyone from creating an object of the class, enforcing its use as a static-only utility.

Declaring the class as abstract would also prevent instantiation, but it is a misleading and poor design choice. A class should only be abstract if it is meant to be extended, which is not the purpose of a static utility class.

The pitfalls of using 'abstract' include: it suggests the class has a purpose in an inheritance hierarchy, it can still have non-static members which are inaccessible, and it is semantically incorrect as the class is not a template for subclasses but a container for helper functions.
